data.title: Donnelley Financial targets 30%+ adjusted EBITDA margin by 2028 amid software growth
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Donnelley Financial Solutions (NYSE:DFIN) Q4 2024

Management View

  • CEO Dan Leib highlighted the company’s strategy execution, emphasizing strong growth in its software solutions, which achieved a 13.8% organic net sales increase for the year. Software solutions now represent 42.2% of total net sales, driven by products like ActiveDisclosure and Arc Suite.

  • The company reported a decline in event-driven transactional revenue by $20 million or 33% year-over-year for the quarter due to muted deal activity. Despite this, full-year adjusted EBITDA reached $217.3 million, representing a 4.8% year-over-year increase and a margin of 27.8%.

  • CFO Dave Gardella noted, "Our fourth quarter adjusted EBITA was $31.7 million, reflecting a 20.3% margin," primarily impacted by lower transactional revenue but partly offset by cost controls and software growth.

Outlook

  • Management expects Q1 2025 consolidated net sales between $190 million and $200 million, with an adjusted EBITDA margin in the mid-20% range. Capital markets transactional sales are anticipated to decrease slightly to $45 million.

  • The company reiterated its long-term goal of achieving an adjusted EBITDA margin of 30% or higher by 2028, supported by its strategic focus on software solutions and cost management.

Financial Results

  • Total Q4 2024 net sales were $156.3 million, an 11.4% decrease from the previous year, largely due to a $20 million decline in event-driven transactional revenue. Software solutions net sales, however, grew 11.6% organically.

  • Adjusted non-GAAP gross margin for the quarter was 59.9%, a slight improvement over Q4 2023. Adjusted non-GAAP SG&A expenses decreased by $2.1 million, though its percentage of net sales rose due to lower overall revenue.

  • Free cash flow for the year reached $105.2 million, up $43 million from 2023, driven by EBITDA improvements and favorable working capital.

Q&A

  • Charles Strauzer, CJS Securities: Asked about the variance between Q4 results and guidance. CFO Gardella attributed the shortfall to a $10 million miss in capital markets transactional revenue, which typically has incremental margins of 50%-60%, significantly impacting EBITDA.

  • Kyle Peterson, Needham: Inquired about the outlook for de-SPAC activity. Craig Clay explained the company is deprioritizing low-quality deals, focusing instead on high-quality opportunities, and expects a normalization by 2025 or 2026.

  • Charles Strauzer, CJS Securities: Followed up on Q1 guidance assumptions. Gardella noted confidence in the $45 million transactional revenue target, citing visibility into January and February activity, though acknowledging variability remains.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed cautious optimism, raising concerns over transactional revenue shortfalls and competitive pressures in print and distribution. The de-SPAC strategy pivot and regulatory shifts were key topics.

  • Management maintained a confident tone during prepared remarks, highlighting strategic wins and cost discipline. However, during Q&A, defensive language was noted when addressing revenue challenges and market uncertainties.

  • Compared to Q3 2024, management’s tone was consistent, with a focus on long-term goals, though analysts appeared slightly more skeptical about near-term market conditions.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q4 2024 guidance was realized with transactional revenue challenges, aligning with management’s earlier caution. Compared to Q3 2024, software solutions continued to grow, albeit at a slightly moderated pace.

  • Capital markets transactional revenue declined further, while software products like ActiveDisclosure and Venue achieved double-digit growth.

  • Management’s sentiment remained steady, emphasizing the company’s transformation, while analysts in Q4 focused more on specific headwinds like de-SPAC activity and print revenue declines.

Risks and Concerns

  • Prolonged weakness in transactional revenue due to muted deal activity and macroeconomic uncertainties.

  • Competition in the print and distribution segment, particularly with aggressive pricing strategies from peers.

  • Potential headwinds in regulatory-driven revenue streams, as some segments face reductions in mandatory filings.

Final Takeaway

Donnelley Financial Solutions demonstrated resilience in Q4 2024, driven by software solutions growth and cost controls, despite significant challenges in transactional revenue. Management’s focus on achieving a 30%+ adjusted EBITDA margin by 2028 remains intact, supported by strategic investments in SaaS offerings and operational efficiency. The company’s cautious Q1 2025 guidance reflects continued market uncertainties, particularly in transactional activity, but also highlights optimism for long-term software-led growth.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Fed's Barr: Firms heavily invested in gen AI could see stock corrections
data.description:
In a hypothetical scenario where generative artificial intelligence (AI) adds only incremental progress to human development, there is a risk that its expected returns will ultimately be seen as "overhyped" and will lead to corrections in stocks of companies that have spent heavily on it, the Federal Reserve's Michael Barr said on Tuesday.

The central bank's Vice Chair for Supervision explored two hypothetical scenarios for generative AI's impact on humans in prepared remarks at the Council on Foreign Relations in New York.

"In one (scenario), we see only incremental adoption that primarily augments what humans do today, but still leads to widespread productivity gains. In the other, we see transformative change where we extend human capabilities with far-reaching consequences. For each scenario, I consider the potential implications for the economy and financial sector," Barr said.

In the first scenario of just incremental progress, the Fed's Vice Chair sees a risk that the "expected value creation" from generative AI will not have been what was hoped for.

"This could trigger market corrections for the firms that have heavily invested in this technology if reality doesn't measure up to expectations. While the U.S. economy experienced a surge of productivity growth during the dot.com boom in the late 1990s, it was followed by a wave of bankruptcies, capital overhang, and a cautious business investment climate," he said.

Megacap firms such as Facebook-owner Meta Platforms (META), Google-parent Alphabet (GOOG)(GOOGL), and OpenAI-backer Microsoft (MSFT) have said that they anticipate spending tens of billions of dollars on capital expenditure in 2025 and beyond to drive AI processes. The AI community as a whole received a shock in late January when Chinese startup DeepSeek released a low-cost model, causing investors to relook at U.S. companies' massive spending plans.

Barr's second hypothetical scenario imagines a world "in which GenAI adoption extends beyond improving on what we currently do, and provides new expertise and capabilities that have transformative effects on the economy and society."

"Although this transformative scenario is more speculative and is accompanied by a far greater degree of uncertainty than the first, it is important to consider given the extraordinary opportunities for human advancement and welfare that could arise, even if just one of its transformative components were to come to fruition. We would need to fundamentally reimagine how the economy is structured," Barr said.

However, he cautioned that in such a scenario, there were financial risks that could potentially lead to more market unpredictability.

"The very attributes that make GenAI attractive—the speed, automaticity, and ability to optimize financial strategies—also present risk. When the technology becomes ubiquitous, use of GenAI could lead to herding behavior and the concentration of risk, potentially amplifying market volatility," the Fed's Vice Chair said.

"As GenAI agents will be directed to maximize profit, they may converge on strategies to maximize returns through coordinated market manipulation, potentially fueling asset bubbles and crashes. Speed, automaticity, and ubiquity could generate new risks at wide scale," he added.

Here are some popular exchange-traded funds that track AI and robotics: (NASDAQ:AIQ), (BOTZ), (DTEC), (GINN), (IRBO), (ROBT), (TECB), (XT), (THNQ), and (CHAT).

data.title: Where to direct money after selling Mag 7 exposure: Trivariate
data.description:
Health care is among three main areas worthy of investment consideration after trimming exposure to Magnificent 7 stocks, Trivariate Research said.

Relatively expensive valuation, increasing capital spending-to-sales, and above-normal potential for disruption were among Trivariate’s reasons last week to suggest that investors reduce exposure to the tech heavyweights.

The firm this week said with the money raised through selling some Mag 7 exposure, investors should primarily look to select Health Care (XLV), Industrials (XLI), and Capital Markets-sensitive stocks:

Here’s a summary of Trivariate’s views:

  • Healthcare: The sector has “very consistent” revenue per share growth. Analyst estimates and recommendations embed optimism across each sub-industry despite headlines about potential government-led cost-cutting, Trivariate said.
  • “We are attracted to the potential for AI-induced productivity driving higher margins,” Trivariate Founder Adam Parker said. High-quality growth healthcare stocks include Boston Scientific (NYSE:BSX), Stryker (NYSE:SYK), McKesson (NYSE:MCK), and Veeva Systems (NYSE:VEEV), the firm said.
  • Industrials: Trivariate said its proprietary Industrial Activity Gauge is increasing, and the sector is “ripe" for corporate action.
  • “While we — like everyone else — are attracted to the Electrification theme, we saw the high correlation of that theme’s poster child – Eaton (NYSE:ETN) to AI Semiconductors on the ‘DeepSeek Monday,’ Parker said.
  • Investors should also look at multiple Industrial end-markets, including Aerospace & Defense, Machinery, Conglomerates, and Electrical Equipment, which could cyclically benefit in 2025.
  • Capital Markets: “We continue to want to own Financials that benefit from capital markets activity,” which includes JPMorgan (NYSE:JPM), Blackstone (NYSE:BX), Apollo (NYSE:APO), Evercore (NYSE:EVR), Morgan Stanley (NYSE:MS) and Goldman Sachs (NYSE:GS), Trivariate said. “We are believers,” it said.

Here's how the Mag 7 stocks are performing year-to-date through Tuesday afternoon:

  • Alphabet (GOOG)(GOOGL): down -3.2%
  • Amazon: (AMZN): up +2.3%
  • Apple (AAPL): down -3.1%
  • Meta (META) up +21.5%
  • Nvidia (NVDA): up +4.4%
  • Microsoft (MSFT): down -3.3%
  • Tesla (TSLA): down -12.5%

data.title: Noble Corporation outlines $3.25B-$3.45B revenue guidance for 2025 amid demand rebound projections
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Noble Corporation (NYSE:NE) Q4 2024

Management View

  • CEO Robert Eifler highlighted the completion of the Diamond Offshore acquisition, which bolsters Noble's position in deepwater drilling. The integration has already realized half of the $100 million synergy target, with full realization expected by year-end 2025.
  • During Q4 2024, Noble achieved adjusted EBITDA of $319 million and paid $80 million in dividends while repurchasing $50 million in shares, summing up to over $575 million of capital returns for the year.
  • The company announced a $0.50 dividend for Q1 2025, bringing cumulative dividends and buybacks since Q4 2022 to over $900 million.
  • Contract wins totaling over $500 million in firm commitments were secured, strengthening the 2025 and 2026 contract coverage. Tier-1 drillships saw day rates stabilize in the mid-to-high $400,000 range.
  • CFO Richard Barker reported Q4 contract drilling services revenue at $882 million and cash flow from operations of $136 million. Total backlog stood at $5.8 billion as of February 17, including $2.4 billion scheduled for 2025.

Outlook

  • Noble provided full-year 2025 guidance, projecting revenue between $3.25 billion and $3.45 billion, adjusted EBITDA of $1.05 billion to $1.15 billion, and capital expenditures of $375 million to $425 million.
  • Management anticipates Q1 2025 adjusted EBITDA to decline approximately 5% quarter-over-quarter, excluding the Q4 2024 contract termination impact.
  • Noble remains focused on expanding its high-spec fleet utilization, expecting demand for ultra-deepwater rigs to rebound to 105-110 units by late 2026 or 2027, despite current market softness.

Financial Results

  • Q4 2024 adjusted EBITDA margin was 34%, positively impacted by $40 million from the early termination of the Noble Deliverer. Free cash flow was constrained to $2 million due to transaction costs and a temporary working capital increase.
  • For the full year 2024, Noble generated $3.1 billion in revenue and $1.1 billion in adjusted EBITDA. Lower capital expenditures projected for 2025 signal an improvement in free cash flow after peaking at $550 million in 2024.

Q&A

  • Arun Jayaram, JPMorgan: Questioned the outlook for tier-1 drillship opportunities. CEO Eifler noted active discussions globally, with opportunities spanning the “golden triangle” regions and contracts potentially commencing in 2025 and 2026.
  • Eddie Kim, Barclays: Asked about the decision to retire the Meltem drillship. Eifler explained the diminished near-term demand for stacked capacity and limited profitability scenarios led to the decision, which will save $20 million annually in stacking costs.
  • Fredrik Stene, Clarksons Securities: Inquired about fleet optimization and utilization improvement. CFO Barker detailed that approximately 90% of 2025 revenue guidance is supported by existing contracts and options, while CEO Eifler emphasized the intervention market potential for Globetrotter rigs.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed skepticism over short-term utilization gaps but acknowledged management’s proactive measures to optimize fleet operations. Questions reflected concerns over delayed demand recovery and rig retirements.
  • Management maintained a slightly positive tone during prepared remarks, emphasizing long-term demand stability and opportunities in 2026 and beyond. However, responses during Q&A were more cautious, reflecting the current market softness and uncertainties.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q4 2024 saw stronger capital returns and contract wins compared to Q3 2024, though utilization gaps and reduced near-term demand for stacked rigs persisted.
  • Management’s tone remained consistent regarding long-term optimism, but Q4 highlighted strategic rig retirements and deeper focus on optimizing the fleet.
  • Analysts’ focus shifted from integration progress in Q3 to utilization challenges and demand recovery in Q4.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management outlined risks associated with a mid-cycle lull in deepwater demand persisting into 2025, driven by upstream capital discipline and a comfortably supplied oil market.
  • Analyst concerns centered around potential delays in customer commitments and the impact of soft demand on EBITDA growth in 2025.
  • Noble’s mitigation strategies include retiring underperforming rigs, focusing on high-spec fleet utilization, and pursuing intervention market opportunities.

Final Takeaway

Noble Corporation is navigating near-term market softness by focusing on high-spec fleet optimization, disciplined capital returns, and realizing synergies from the Diamond Offshore acquisition. While 2025 guidance reflects a modestly cautious outlook, management remains confident in demand recovery by 2026, supported by active discussions for long-term contracts and a robust backlog of $5.8 billion. Strategic retirements and cost-saving initiatives position the company to capitalize on future opportunities in the deepwater market.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Gilead upped on HIV franchise; Merck downgraded on Keytruda LoE at Deutsche Bank
data.description:
Deutsche Bank upgraded Gilead Sciences (NASDAQ:GILD) on Tuesday, citing the antiviral developer’s HIV treatment franchise, and downgraded Merck (NYSE:MRK), citing the cancer drugmaker’s upcoming loss of exclusivity for its blockbuster therapy Keytruda.

Assuming five long-acting HIV drugs, including GILD’s upcoming HIV-1 capsid inhibitor Lenacapavir in the company’s pipeline, the analyst James Shin upgraded the stock to Buy from Hold after raising estimates for GILD’s financials for 2029 and beyond.

The nearly $19B extra revenue attributed to this addition drastically changes the firm’s previous thesis, which predicted a potential revenue decline following the patent cliff for GILD's HIV treatment Biktarvy in late 2033.

“For Biktarvy’s 2033 patent cliff, we believe GILD’s pipeline of long-acting orals and injectables can combine for ~$19B of HIV treatment revenues by FY2035,” Shin wrote while raising his price on GILD to $120 from $80 per share.

The upgrade coincided with the FDA’s decision to grant priority review for lenacapavir as a PrEP therapy for HIV prevention this week.

Meanwhile, Shin downgraded MRK to Hold from Buy and lowered its price target to $105 from $128 per share, arguing the “real culprit” behind the stock’s selloff since the recent earnings print is Keytruda’s loss of exclusivity in 2028.

“Despite MRK building out its pipeline, past precedence shows the Street is very skeptical when mega blockbusters face loss of exclusivity (LOE),” the analyst wrote, referring to AbbVie’s (ABBV) arthritis drug Humira, which faced its U.S. LoE in early 2023.

“AbbVie (ABBV) got a lot right, yet it took a while for the Street to come around,” Shin argued, adding that ABBV shares also remained volatile in the lead-up to Humira LoE before entering a lengthy recovery period.

data.title: FDIC said to axe probationary staff as Trump seeks to shrink federal workforce
data.description:
The Federal Deposit Insurance Corp. reportedly fired an unspecified number of probationary employees on Monday, following voluntary buyouts under the Trump administration's federal-workforce-reduction efforts that have already reduced the top banking regulator's workforce by around 500.

There were 513 FDIC employees with a tenure of less than one year, according to the Department of Government Efficiency website.

While some estimates inflate the count of probationary FDIC staff, there are indications that those who transferred from other agencies could be exempt from the layoffs, Bloomberg reported, citing multiple unidentified sources.

Meanwhile, the FDIC confirmed on Feb. 14 that some 8% of the agency's total workforce, or around 500 employees, accepted deferred resignation buyouts under the White House's initiative, the article said, noting the FDIC had about 6,300 employees before the buyouts and layoffs.

Last week, four Democratic U.S. Senators asked the FDIC's Office of the Inspector General to conduct a review to determine whether the regulator's acting chairman's reported decision to rescind more than 200 job offers to bank examiners threatens the banking system's stability.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: UFP Industries outlines $60M cost savings by 2026 with new product expansion
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: UFP Industries (NASDAQ:UFPI) Q4 2024

Management View

  • UFP Industries achieved $6.7 billion in sales for 2024, with an EBITDA of $682.3 million, reflecting a 10.3% margin. The company also reported a strong balance sheet, with $1.2 billion in cash and equivalent debt capacity, supporting growth strategies and M&A opportunities.
  • CEO William Schwartz outlined a focus on profitable growth and identified $60 million in structural cost savings targeted by 2026. He emphasized the company’s commitment to expanding its presence through acquisitions, greenfield projects, and product innovation.
  • The company launched Summit Decking, utilizing proprietary Surestone technology, aimed at the DIY and small contractor markets. The product will roll out to over 1,500 stores in 2025, supported by a marketing campaign to enhance brand awareness.
  • CFO Mike Cole highlighted a 4% decline in Q4 sales to $1.46 billion, driven by weaker demand and pricing pressures, which led to a 28% drop in adjusted EBITDA to $133 million, with a 9.1% margin.

Outlook

  • Management expects challenging business conditions from 2024 to persist through the first half of 2025, with modest unit declines anticipated across all segments.
  • The company remains cautious due to uncertainties in existing home sales, housing affordability, and mixed industry forecasts. It foresees growth in the back half of 2025 as conditions stabilize.
  • Tariffs on Canadian lumber and pricing inflation are expected to impact wood species costs, but management is confident in mitigating these challenges through purchasing strategies.
  • UFP Industries continues to target long-term growth through investments in automation, new product development, and capacity expansion.

Financial Results

  • Q4 revenue declined to $1.46 billion, with a 4% reduction in selling prices and flat unit sales. Adjusted EBITDA decreased to $133 million, reflecting a 9.1% margin.
  • Retail sales were flat at $525 million, supported by a 20% increase in decking sales, driven by the Surestone product line, which now accounts for 55% of composite decking sales.
  • Packaging sales dropped 9% to $375 million, impacted by an 8% decline in selling prices and a 2% decrease in units. Structural Packaging experienced the most significant volume decline.
  • Construction sales fell 5% to $487 million, with factory-built and concrete-forming units showing volume increases, offset by declines in site-built and commercial units.
  • Operating cash flow for the year totaled $643 million, while capital expenditures were $232 million, primarily allocated to automation, geographic expansion, and manufacturing capacity.

Q&A

  • Kurt Yinger, D.A. Davidson: Asked about Deckorators shelf space changes and Pro expansion strategies. CEO Schwartz stated that Summit Decking will have significant placement in over 1,500 stores by the second half of 2025.
  • Ketan Mamtora, BMO Capital Markets: Inquired about pricing trends in Packaging. CEO Missad noted continued competitive pressures but stated that pricing appears to have stabilized.
  • Reuben Garner, Benchmark: Asked about lumber tariffs and potential adoption of Southern Yellow Pine. CEO Schwartz confirmed that species adoption based on pricing trends is already underway.
  • James McCanless, Wedbush Securities: Questioned cost-saving measures and the trajectory of factory-built housing. CFO Cole detailed $41 million in cost-saving actions identified for 2025 and highlighted strong growth prospects in factory-built housing.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed cautious skepticism, focusing on pricing pressures, cost-saving measures, and market stabilization. Questions reflected concerns over demand softness and competitive dynamics.
  • Management maintained a confident tone, emphasizing long-term growth strategies and operational efficiencies. CEO Schwartz highlighted the company’s resilience and strategic investments to navigate near-term challenges.
  • Compared to Q3, analysts demonstrated a slightly more pressing tone regarding cost management and market uncertainties, while management appeared slightly more cautious in discussing short-term demand and pricing trends.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The $60 million cost-saving target for 2026 represents a refinement from the $70 million target in Q3, reflecting progress in identifying actionable measures.
  • Strategic focus shifted towards product development, with the launch of Summit Decking and increased investments in new product innovation.
  • Management’s tone in Q4 was cautiously optimistic, compared to a more defensive stance in Q3, reflecting ongoing challenges in demand and pricing.

Risks and Concerns

  • Continued softness in demand and competitive pricing pressures in Packaging and Construction segments.
  • Potential impact of Canadian lumber tariffs on material costs.
  • Limited visibility beyond the first half of 2025 due to mixed industry forecasts and broader economic uncertainties.
  • Management highlighted mitigation strategies, including cost containment, capacity reductions, and diversification through acquisitions and product innovation.

Final Takeaway

UFP Industries is navigating a challenging market environment with a focus on long-term growth through cost reductions, product innovation, and strategic investments. While near-term visibility remains limited, the company is leveraging its strong balance sheet and diversified portfolio to position itself for improved profitability and sustained shareholder value.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Amazon closes its TikTok-like service for shopping - report
data.description:
Amazon (NASDAQ:AMZN) has shuttered its TikTok-style shopping service, Inspire, The Information reported Tuesday, citing messages to users and a company spokesperson. The service launched in 2022 and was available in the mobile app.

“We regularly evaluate various features to better align with what customers tell us matters most, and as part of that, Inspire is no longer available,” an Amazon spokesperson reportedly said.

In the navigation tab at the bottom of the Amazon app, a lightbulb-shaped icon allowed users to access the Inspire feed. The feed featured an endless scroll of videos and images of products sold on Amazon.

The feed showed products promoted by influencers who received commissions for their posts and from customers’ video reviews on Amazon.

data.title: Fiverr launches a new AI platform for its creators
data.description:
Fiverr International (NYSE:FVRR) announced on Tuesday the launch of Fiverr Go, which the company described as an innovative AI platform that redefines the relationship between human creativity and artificial intelligence. The initiative is aimed at protecting and amplifying creator rights, while helping them scale their businesses in an era where AI is seen as often exploiting human creativity without proper attribution or compensation.

Fiverr Go is not just another AI platform, according to CEO Micha Kaufman, but a fundamental reimagining of how AI and human creativity can work together. The platform offers a Personal AI Creation Model that allows creators to train AI exclusively on their own work, maintaining control over their creative process and rights. There is also a Personal AI Assistant that analyzes past interactions to become uniquely tailored to each freelancer's preferences, enhancing communication with potential clients, handling routine tasks, and providing business insights.

At launch, Fiverr Go will be available across several creative service categories, including voiceover, songwriting, graphic design, illustration, copywriting, and digital marketing. Initial access will be limited to top, vetted freelancers with proven track records of creative excellence on the platform. Importantly, Fiverr Go will also be an open platform for developers, allowing them to develop real-world AI applications running on Fiverr's extensive transactional data set.

Additionally, Fiverr (NYSE:FVRR) announced its upcoming Freelancer Equity Program, noted to be an industry-first initiative designed to give top-performing freelancers shares in the company.

data.title: Former OpenAI CTO launches new AI startup Thinking Machines Lab
data.description:
Former OpenAI Chief Technology Officer Mira Murati said on Tuesday that she has launched a new artificial intelligence startup, Thinking Machines Lab.

"I started Thinking Machines Lab alongside a remarkable team of scientists, engineers, and builders," Murati wrote in a post on X.

She continued: "We're building three things: Helping people adapt AI systems to work for their specific needs; Developing strong foundations to build more capable AI systems; Fostering a culture of open science that helps the whole field understand and improve these systems."

The 36-year-old Murati, who stepped down from the Microsoft (NASDAQ:MSFT) backed OpenAI in September, said the goal of the new startup is to "advance AI by making it broadly useful and understandable through solid foundations, open science, and practical applications."

"We plan to frequently publish technical blog posts, papers, and code," Thinking Machines Lab wrote on its website. "We think sharing our work will not only benefit the public, but also improve our own research culture."

In addition to Murati, former OpenAI executives at Thinking Machines Lab include John Schulman, who co-founded the ChatGPT maker in 2015 and Barret Zoph. Schulman will be Thinking Machine's chief scientist, while Zoph, who also left OpenAI in September, will be its CTO.

Several other former OpenAI employees are listed on Thinking Machine's website, as are former Google (GOOG) (GOOGL) and Meta (META) employees.

Thinking Machines Lab has not raised any funding yet, nor does it have any products just yet. However, Bloomberg reported that Murati had spoken to venture capital firms and was seeking to raise roughly $1B, citing a person familiar with the matter.

Separately on Tuesday, it was reported that Safe Superintelligence, an AI startup from former OpenAI chief scientist Ilya Sutskever, had raised more than $1B in new funding that valued the company at $30B.
